title: Diabetes
tagline: Control blood sugar, control your life
oneLiner: Diabetes means your blood sugar stays too high because your body can't use insulin properly or doesn't make enough.

cards.title: What is Blood Sugar?
cards.body: When you eat, your body breaks food into glucose (sugar) and sends it into your blood. Insulin — a hormone made by your pancreas — acts like a key that lets glucose enter your cells for energy.

cards.title: The Insulin Problem
cards.body: In Type 2 diabetes (most common), cells stop responding to insulin — this is called insulin resistance. In Type 1, the pancreas barely makes any insulin at all.

cards.title: Why Sugar Rises
cards.body: When insulin can't do its job, glucose piles up in the bloodstream instead of entering cells. Eating sugary or high-carb food in big amounts causes rapid spikes.

cards.title: Rice, Roti & GI
cards.body: White rice and maida (refined wheat) digest fast, flooding your blood with glucose quickly. Choosing brown rice, millets, or whole-wheat roti in smaller portions slows this spike.

cards.title: 3 Types of Carbs
cards.body: Not all carbs behave the same. Sugars (simple carbs) digest fast and spike blood sugar quickly — found in sweets, juice, and packaged foods. Starches (complex carbs) are long chains of sugar — wholegrain versions digest slower. Fiber cannot be digested at all — it slows glucose entry into your blood.

cards.title: Fiber: The Hidden Protector
cards.body: Dietary fiber forms a physical barrier in your gut that slows glucose absorption. Soluble fiber — found in oats, rajma, dal, apples, and fenugreek — is especially powerful. Eating carbs alongside fiber-rich foods can reduce post-meal blood sugar spikes by 20–30%.

cards.title: The ¼ Plate Rule
cards.body: Build every meal like this: ½ plate non-starchy vegetables (spinach, cucumber, bhindi, tomato) → ¼ plate starchy carbs (rice, roti, potato) → ¼ plate protein (dal, eggs, paneer, fish). Eat in this order too — vegetables first, carbs last. This sequence alone cuts post-meal spikes significantly.

cards.title: Exercise is Like a Second Insulin
cards.body: Physical activity makes your muscles absorb glucose directly — even without insulin. A 30-minute walk after meals can cut post-meal sugar spikes significantly.

cards.title: Meal Timing Matters
cards.body: Long gaps between meals let blood sugar drop dangerously in people on medication. Eating smaller meals every 3–4 hours keeps levels stable through the day.

cards.title: Pre-diabetes is Reversible
cards.body: Pre-diabetes means sugar is higher than normal but not yet diabetic range. Diet changes and weight loss of even 5–7% of body weight can completely reverse it.

cards.title: Sleep & Stress Raise Sugar
cards.body: Poor sleep and stress release cortisol, which raises blood glucose. Managing stress through yoga, breathing exercises, or 7–8 hours of sleep directly helps diabetes control.

doEat.name: Dal & legumes
doEat.note: High protein + fibre, slow carb absorption

doEat.name: Leafy greens
doEat.note: Very low carb, high in magnesium

doEat.name: Whole fruits (not juice)
doEat.note: Fibre slows sugar release; avoid mango/banana excess

doEat.name: Sweet potato (shakarkandi)
doEat.note: Lower GI than white potato; skin adds extra fibre

doEat.name: Steel-cut / rolled oats
doEat.note: Beta-glucan fibre; very slow, steady glucose rise

doEat.name: Barley (jau)
doEat.note: Lowest GI grain; forms gel in gut that blunts glucose spike

dontEat.name: Sugary drinks & fruit juice
dontEat.note: Sugar enters blood instantly with no fibre

dontEat.name: Alcohol
dontEat.note: Unpredictable sugar swings, especially on medication

dontEat.name: White bread / pav / burger bun
dontEat.note: Refined flour, zero fibre — GI almost as high as pure sugar

dontEat.name: Instant noodles (Maggi / Top Ramen)
dontEat.note: Maida base + high sodium — fast glucose spike, zero nutrition

lifestyle: Walk for at least 30 minutes after your largest meal — reduces post-meal glucose spike by up to 30%.
Check your blood sugar at the same time daily to spot patterns (morning fasting + 2 hours after meals).
Reduce portion size of rice/roti by 25% and fill the rest with vegetable sabzi or dal.
Avoid eating fruit as juice — always eat whole fruit with the fibre.
Sleep 7–8 hours. Even one bad night's sleep increases insulin resistance the next day.
Practice 5-minute deep breathing or meditation daily to lower cortisol (stress hormone that raises sugar).
Stay hydrated — dehydration concentrates blood glucose. Aim for 8–10 glasses of water daily.
Eat vegetables before your rice or roti at every meal — 'veggie-first' order reduces post-meal glucose spike by up to 30%.
Pair every carb with protein or fat: rice with dal, roti with paneer or egg, oats with nuts. Protein and fat slow down carb digestion.
Spread your carb intake evenly across all 3 meals — eating all your carbs in one sitting (e.g., heavy dinner) causes a large single spike.
redFlags: Blood sugar above 300 mg/dL or you feel confused or very drowsy
Sudden weakness, shakiness, sweating — may be dangerously low blood sugar (hypoglycaemia)
Chest pain, breathlessness, or leg swelling — diabetes affects the heart and kidneys
Foot wounds that are not healing or turning black
Blurred vision that has worsened over weeks
Frequent urination at night combined with extreme thirst

quiz.q: What does insulin do in the body?
options: Lets glucose enter your cells for energy
quiz.why: Insulin is the 'key' that unlocks cells so glucose from food can enter and be used as fuel.

quiz.q: Fruit juice is healthier than whole fruit for a diabetic.
quiz.why: Juice removes the fibre, so sugar enters the bloodstream very quickly. Whole fruit is much better.

quiz.q: Which food has the lowest glycemic impact?
quiz.why: Brown rice and millets digest slower, causing a gradual glucose rise instead of a spike.

quiz.q: A 30-minute walk after meals can help reduce blood sugar spikes.
quiz.why: Muscles absorb glucose directly during exercise, working like a second insulin — very effective post-meal.

quiz.q: Which type of carbohydrate helps slow down blood sugar rise after a meal?
quiz.why: Fiber cannot be digested — it forms a slow-down barrier in your gut that reduces the speed of glucose absorption, giving a gentler blood sugar curve.
